items.q: What is the difference between qualitative and quantitative data?
items.correct: Quantitative data is numerical and measurable (traffic counts, river width); qualitative data is descriptive and non-numerical (interview quotes, photo descriptions, field sketches).
items.misconception: Quantitative data is always more scientific and reliable than qualitative.
items.random_wrong: Qualitative data means high-quality data and quantitative means a lot of data.
items.why: Both have value: numbers show patterns; words explain why. Mixed-methods studies combine them. Students wrongly assume numbers are objective and words are subjective — both can be done well or poorly.

items.q: What is the ITCZ and why does it bring rain?
items.correct: The Intertropical Convergence Zone is where trade winds from both hemispheres converge near the equator, forcing air to rise, cool and produce heavy convectional rainfall.
items.misconception: The ITCZ is a fixed band of permanent storms at the equator.
items.random_wrong: The ITCZ is the boundary between the Atlantic and Pacific oceans.
items.why: The ITCZ shifts seasonally north and south with the sun, driving monsoon rains. Students treat it as static — its movement explains why tropical regions have wet/dry seasons.

items.q: What is the difference between glacial abrasion and plucking?
items.correct: Abrasion: rock embedded in the glacier base scrapes the bedrock smooth, creating striations. Plucking: meltwater refreezes onto bedrock, then movement rips chunks of rock away.
items.misconception: Abrasion and plucking are different words for the same process.
items.random_wrong: Abrasion is when wind erodes the glacier and plucking is when rain does.
items.why: Both processes act together but produce different evidence: abrasion leaves smooth scratched surfaces; plucking leaves jagged surfaces. Students conflate them — recognising striations vs roches moutonnees is exam-critical.

items.q: What is the main driver of Amazon deforestation?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Cattle ranching. “Mining” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Emergent. “Canopy” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Roughly what percentage of the Amazon has been lost since 1970?
items.why: Exact answer: 17 %. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
